metadata.title: Causal Inference — Causal Agent Supply Chain
metadata.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CAUSAL AGENT SUPPLY CHAIN
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(slate) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro. Two-stage causal agent: classify regime-confounded vs genuine-stress, then route to intervention or diagnostic.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: A supply chain alert arrives. Is it a genuine stress event or a regime-confounded reading — seasonal noise, a supplier holiday, a known pattern? Acting on confounded alerts costs real money: expedited shipments, emergency sourcing, team mobilization. A two-stage causal agent classifies the alert before routing — stage one decides whether the reading is genuine or confounded, stage two routes to the right response.
beats.visual_intent: title card: alert arrives -> Stage 1 classify (genuine-stress vs regime-confounded vs unknown) -> Stage 2 route (intervention vs diagnostic); the cost of acting on confounded alerts

beats.narration_text: Into the terminal: claude — build a Python two-stage causal agent for supply chain risk: stage one classifies an alert as regime-confounded, genuine-stress, or unknown; stage two routes based on classification — genuine gets an intervention, regime-confounded gets a diagnostic; print a structured decision tree output.

props.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CAUSAL AGENT SUPPLY CHAIN

beats.narration_text: Read it. Stage one is the classification call: given the alert text, Claude returns the causal classification and a confidence score. Stage two branches on that: genuine-stress triggers the intervention pipeline, regime-confounded triggers the diagnostic pipeline, and unknown escalates to a human.

props.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CAUSAL AGENT SUPPLY CHAIN

beats.narration_text: Run it on three test alerts. First: high sensor reading during monsoon season — regime-confounded, routes to diagnostic. Second: sudden supplier dropout, no seasonal pattern — genuine-stress, routes to intervention. Third: borderline reading in transition period — unknown, escalates to human review.
beats.visual_intent: screen-recording or slate: terminal showing three alerts classified and routed correctly; structured decision tree output with classification, confidence, and action for each

beats.narration_text: The unknown bucket is too large — thirty percent of alerts escalate. Iterate: add a second Claude call for unknown alerts that searches for historical analogues and resolves the classification using the nearest regime-matched precedent.

props.command:
claude "update causal_agent.py:
  -> for unknown classification, add Stage 1b:
     search historical analogues, find nearest
     regime-matched precedent, resolve classification
  -> target: reduce unknown rate below 10%"
props.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CAUSAL AGENT SUPPLY CHAIN

beats.narration_text: Re-run. The borderline reading matches a historical precedent from the same regime — reclassified as regime-confounded, routed to diagnostic. Unknown rate drops to eight percent. The agent now resolves most ambiguous alerts through analogue retrieval.
beats.visual_intent: screen-recording or slate: terminal showing Stage 1b analogue retrieval resolving the borderline alert; unknown rate reported as 8%

beats.narration_text: The two-stage architecture is causal hygiene at the operational level. Stage one asks: is this signal genuine or confounded? Stage two asks: what response does that classification warrant? Without stage one, every alert gets the same intervention — and most are confounded noise.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: two-stage pipeline diagram — Stage 1 classify -> Stage 2 route; 'classify before you act' as the causal principle

beats.narration_text: Your move: audit your current alert response pipeline — does it have a classification stage before the intervention stage? If not, you are routing by signal strength alone, not causal type. Add the classification step and measure how many interventions were actually warranted.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) audit your alert pipeline for classification stage, 2) classify by causal type not signal strength, 3) measure warranted vs confounded interventions

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
